figures.caption: TABLE I QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON WITH REPRESENTATIVE SHADOW DETECTION METHODS ON THREE BENCHMARKS. “BER” DENOTES THE BALANCED ERROR RATE. “S” AND “NS” DENOTE THE BER VALUES ON SHADOW AND NON-SHADOW REGIONS, RESPECTIVELY. BEST AND SECOND-BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD AND UNDERLINED, RESPECTIVELY. “↓” INDICATES LOWER IS BETTER.
Fig. 4. Qualitative comparison with representative shadow detection methods. SwinS and SAdapter are short for SwinShadow and ShadowAdapter, respectively.

TABLE IV TEXT-REFERENCE ANALYSIS ON STANDARD AND HARD-CASE EVALUATIONS. “ALL” AND “D20 BER” DENOTE BER OVER ALL PIXELS AND OVER THE DARKEST 20% PIXELS OF EACH IMAGE, RESPECTIVELY. BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD.

Fig. 5. Qualitative comparison with representative shadow detection methods on more challenging cases with shadow–non-shadow ambiguity. From top to bottom: (1) intrinsically dark materials, (2) shadow-adjacent dark regions, and (3) textured dark distractors. SwinS and SAdapter are short for SwinShadow and ShadowAdapter, respectively.

Fig. 2. Overall architecture of SVL. A frozen vision encoder extracts multi-level [CLS] and patch tokens, while a frozen text encoder produces shadow-related text embeddings. SVL computes global and patch-wise consistency cues to inject language-guided information into dense prediction, applies auxiliary and scene-level supervision, and uses a lightweight fusion decoder to output the final shadow mask.

TABLE III HARD-CASE SUBSET EVALUATION. SBU-HARD AND UCF-HARD DENOTE THE HARD-CASE SUBSETS CONSTRUCTED FROM SBU AND UCF, RESPECTIVELY. WE REPORT BER OVER ALL PIXELS (ALL: BER↓) AND WITHIN THE DARKEST 20% PIXELS OF EACH IMAGE (D20: BER↓, FPR↓, PREC↑). BEST RESULTS ARE HIGHLIGHTED IN BOLD.

Fig. 6. Sensitivity analysis of the low-level skip feature. We report ∆BER on SBU, UCF, and ISTD when the skip feature is taken from the patch embedding (P) or from different transformer blocks (0–23). ∆BER is computed relative to layer 2, and lower values indicate better performance.
